skill.name: 4-step-program
skill.description: Coordinator workflow for delegating code changes to agents, requiring PR creation, posted reviews, iteration, and final requirement coverage checks.
skill.summary: Orchestrates agent code work through fix, review, iterate, and present gates.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, blocker, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Markdown workflow examples and GitHub-oriented instructions, not executable code. The real risk is operational: the skill directs agents to run GitHub CLI commands and post PR reviews to GitHub, which can affect repositories when used with credentials.

medium_findings.title: GitHub Actions Require External Commands and Network Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk. The skill instructs agents to read issues with gh commands, inspect PR metadata, create or update PRs, and post reviews to GitHub. This is appropriate for the workflow, but it requires repository credentials and can create remote side effects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited instructions explicitly require GitHub CLI or GitHub MCP operations and posting reviews to GitHub. The risk is clear, but the behavior matches the skill purpose rather than showing malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Static External Command Findings
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for embedded command execution. The static analyzer flagged many backtick-delimited examples and fenced Markdown blocks as Ruby or shell execution, but SKILL.md is documentation that tells an agent what workflow to follow. No executable script file or automatic command runner was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are Markdown fences, diagrams, or pseudo-code examples. They are not parsed by a runtime in this skill package.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for suspicious network destinations. The URLs use placeholder owner and repo paths to demonstrate clickable PR and issue formatting. They do not identify a real exfiltration endpoint or hidden callback.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear inside explicit correct-format examples and use generic owner/repo placeholders. No secret data or automated request is associated with them.

low_findings.title: Blocker and Reconnaissance Patterns Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance. The flagged lines contain descriptive workflow text, Markdown examples, Mermaid terminology, or review wording. I found no evidence of cryptographic code, host enumeration, port scanning, or network probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of these locations found prose and Markdown examples rather than executable security-sensitive behavior. The confidence is high because the package contains only SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Repository Side Effects Through GitHub Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks agents to create or update PRs and post reviews using GitHub tooling. This is not malicious, but users should only run it in repositories where the agent is authorized to make comments or PR changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly require remote GitHub actions. The impact depends on user-granted repository permissions.

content.value_statement: Agent coding tasks can finish before every issue requirement and review concern is resolved. This skill defines a strict loop for delegation, PR review, coverage checks, and final presentation.
actual_capabilities: Defines a four-step fix, review, check, and present workflow for delegated code changes.
Requires agents to create or update pull requests for implementation work.
Requires code reviews to be posted to GitHub before work is considered complete.
Adds a final coverage gate that compares implementation against every issue requirement.
Provides examples for PR links, issue links, review summaries, and coverage tables.
Sets iteration and escalation rules when agents do not reach full quality.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not an executable automation script.
It depends on external agents, GitHub access, and a code-reviewer skill being available.
It may be too strict for exploratory prototypes or low-risk internal changes.
It does not verify repository permissions before asking agents to post reviews.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Issue Fixes
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to delegate a bug fix and require every acceptance criterion to be checked before presentation.

use_cases.title: Enforce PR Review Quality
use_cases.description: Require an agent to post a review to GitHub and iterate until no review suggestions remain.

use_cases.title: Audit Agent Completion Claims
use_cases.description: Compare a completed PR against the issue text before telling a human the work is ready.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Fix Loop
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for one issue and one agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the 4-step-program skill to coordinate a fix for issue #123. Delegate the implementation, require a PR, require a posted review, and report only when the final coverage gate passes.

prompt_templates.title: Check Review Completion
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm that the agent actually posted the review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the 4-step-program skill to inspect the agent result. Verify that the review was posted to the GitHub PR and that no review suggestions remain.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Requirement Coverage Gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for checking issue completeness.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the 4-step-program skill to compare PR changes against every requirement in issue #123. Produce a coverage table and send the agent back for any missing or partial item.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a Full Multi-Iteration Delivery
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for strict release readiness.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the 4-step-program skill to manage the full fix, review, iteration, and presentation loop for this PR. Enforce posted GitHub review, 100% issue coverage, exact PR links, and escalation after five failed iterations.

output_examples.input: Coordinate a fix for an authentication issue with four acceptance criteria.
output_examples.output: The coordinator delegates all four requirements, waits for a PR and posted review, checks that each requirement has evidence, and presents the PR only after all gates pass.

output_examples.input: An agent reports a 9 out of 10 review with two remaining suggestions.
output_examples.output: The coordinator rejects completion, sends the agent back to fix both suggestions, and requires another posted review before presenting the work.

output_examples.input: A PR fixes the main bug but misses one edge case from the issue.
output_examples.output: The coordinator marks coverage as incomplete, lists the missing edge case, and loops back to implementation instead of presenting the PR.

best_practices: Use this workflow only in repositories where the agent has approved GitHub permissions.
Paste the full issue requirements into the delegation prompt before work begins.
Verify PR links, posted reviews, and coverage evidence before presenting results.
anti_patterns: Do not accept a local-only review when the workflow requires a GitHub PR comment.
Do not present work with partial issue coverage, even when tests pass.
Do not use the workflow for tasks where the agent must not create remote repository changes.

faq.question: Does this skill write code itself?
faq.answer: No. It coordinates agents that perform code changes and reviews.

faq.question: Why does it require GitHub PR comments?
faq.answer: The workflow requires visible review evidence on the PR so humans can evaluate the work before merging.

faq.question: Can it be used without GitHub?
faq.answer: Not directly. The instructions assume GitHub issues, pull requests, and review comments.

faq.question: What happens if review quality is below 10 out of 10?
faq.answer: The coordinator sends the agent back to fix every remaining suggestion and repeat the review.

faq.question: What is the final coverage gate?
faq.answer: It is a final check that maps every issue requirement to implementation evidence before presentation.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. It is documentation, but it directs agents to use GitHub tools that can create remote side effects.
